weather: Today in Edmonds: Overcast and cool, 47°F at dawn rising only to the upper 50s. A weak front keeps skies gray, with a 20 % chance of light showers this afternoon—mainly sprinkles. Southeast breeze 5-10 mph flips to light westerly later. Evening stays cloudy, low near 45°F. Dress in layers; a light jacket is plenty.

career.notes: Most districts are now posting for the 2026-27 school year. Everett, Lake Washington, and several others use rolling pool postings—submit early to be considered as vacancies arise. Bellevue Christian School offers the only private-school openings listed. Salary ranges shown are 2025-26 schedules; modest increases are expected for 2026-27. All public-school positions require current (or pending) Washington State teaching certificate with appropriate endorsements.

global_news.findings: The United Arab Emirates announced it will exit OPEC, dealing a significant blow to the oil cartel while US-Iran ceasefire negotiations remain stalled. The UAE's departure comes as the Trump administration appears unlikely to accept Iran's latest offer to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, maintaining the blockade that has sent global oil prices soaring above $100 per barrel.

A $500 million Russian superyacht named Nord, linked to sanctioned billionaire Alexei Mordashov, successfully sailed through the Strait of Hormuz despite the ongoing blockade. The 465-foot vessel traveled from Dubai to Oman over the weekend, one of the few private ships to transit the critical waterway since Iran imposed restrictions. The incident highlights how geopolitical tensions are affecting global shipping routes, with maritime traffic through the Gulf at a fraction of pre-war levels.

The US Department of Homeland Security announced new immigration vetting procedures that will scrutinize applicants for what it labels 'extremist views,' including criticism of Israel and participation in pro-Palestinian protests. The policy raises significant First Amendment concerns as immigration officers are instructed to treat such expressions as 'overwhelmingly negative' factors in green card and naturalization decisions. Civil liberties groups warn this represents an unprecedented attack on free speech rights.

EU antitrust regulators issued new guidelines requiring Google to provide competitors with access to key Android capabilities for their AI services, targeting the company's Gemini AI model dominance. The proposed measures would force Google to allow rival AI assistants to integrate with Android devices and access sensitive hardware permissions, potentially undermining the tech giant's competitive advantage in the European market. Google criticized the intervention as unwarranted and privacy-threatening.

A 31-year-old California man has been charged with attempted assassination after opening fire at the White House Correspondents' Dinner where President Trump was speaking. The suspect, Cole Tomas Allen from Torrance, California, allegedly wanted to kill as many high-level officials as possible. The incident has reignited debates about political violence in America and prompted calls for enhanced security protocols at high-profile events.

intellectual_journals.source: The New York Review of Books
intellectual_journals.findings: Will Alden's long-form dispatch from Los Angeles profiles the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI), a monarchist exile group whose weekly rallies outside the Wilshire Federal Building have become fixtures of both “Tehrangeles” and Republican politics.  Over Easter Sunday at Lake Balboa Park, CPI leaders told Alden they are “waiting for day zero,” the moment when former Crown Prince Reza Pahlavi—living in the U.S. since 1979—will call Iranians into the streets to topple the Islamic Republic.  The piece contrasts the nostalgia of older émigrés with younger hard-liners who want a full Pahlavi re [TRUNCATED]

A joint ProPublica–FRONTLINE investigation documents more than 300 arrests of U.S. citizens during the Trump administration’s immigration sweeps, most on charges of “assaulting or interfering with law enforcement.”  Reporters found that over one-third of these cases collapsed once video evidence contradicted sworn officer statements; prosecutors quietly dismissed charges or lost at trial.  The marquee example is Alejandro Orellana, a Marine veteran and UPS worker whose home was raided by agents and a Fox News crew—yet the supposed conspiracy case evaporated within weeks.  Experts warn that eve [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s essay “Grace Against Gravity” meditates on the physics and poetics of avian flight, weaving together Georgia O’Keeffe’s first airplane epiphany, Rachel Carson’s plea for “indestructible wonder,” and Alan Lightman’s explanation of how a bird’s curved wing generates lift.  Writing from 30,000 feet, Popova argues that the V-formation of migrating geese is not just an aerodynamic hack but a metaphor for shared vulnerability: each bird alternates the energy-draining lead position, embodying “an active surrender to the same immutable laws that make the whole miracle of the universe po [TRUNCATED]

Philosopher Nir Eisikovits warns in Jacobin that the real AI threat is not super-intelligent annihilation but the “technologized subjectivity” produced by everyday chatbot use.  Citing a recent MIT study showing reduced brain activity among habitual users, he argues that outsourcing judgment to LLMs risks a gradual erosion of the autonomous reasoning Kant identified as the core of Enlightenment.  Chatbots deliver “decaffeinated subjectivity”: efficient, frictionless, but ultimately hollow.  Eisikovits contends that democratic movements depend on citizens willing to think for themselves; if we  [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy revisits Marcel Proust’s 1905 essay “On Reading,” originally a preface to Ruskin’s Sesame and Lilies, to defend literature as a path to personal freedom rather than moral improvement.  Proust rejected Ruskin’s utilitarian claim that books make us better people, arguing instead that reading preserves “past sensations” and makes “time tangible.”  Champy extends this to argue that literature’s value lies in its capacity for disinterested attention—an escape from the cost-benefit mindset that infects even Victorian notions of literacy.  Close reading, she concludes, is “a unique form  [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Christopher Isabelle’s new paper “Triadic Relational Ontology: From Primordial Separation to Relational Field” (PhilArchive, 2026-04-23) advances TRO as the necessary successor to his earlier Primordial Separation Ontology: once the bare distinction of “here/not-here” has occurred, a third, non-dyadic term—a relational field—must be actively sustained through tension between poles or intelligibility itself disappears.  Matteo Raffaelli, writing in Modern Theology (2026-02-21), roots contemporary Trinitarian ontology in the Czech TRIERTIUM Research Group’s pansophic reading of Comenius, arguing that the triadic synthesis of sciences, arts, and spirit is the only framework capacious enough for current debates on relational being.  Crossing into physics, Oliver T. Schmidt’s March 2026 Journal of Fluid Mechanics article introduces “triadic orthogonal decomposition,” showing that three-wave interactions (momentum donor, recipient, catalyst) expose hidden nonlinear energy cascades in turbule [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The autonomous-research stack is hardening into production-grade tooling. Google Cloud’s PaperOrchestra (arXiv 2604.05018) shows the state-of-the-art: five specialized agents—literature, outline, figure, citation, and LaTeX formatter—collaborate to turn raw lab notes into submission-ready manuscripts in ~4,000 LLM calls, a 6× efficiency jump over earlier “AI Scientist” pipelines. Los Alamos just open-sourced URSA, a multi-agent science swarm that brainstorms hypotheses, designs experiments, and iterates on code; early runs on cosmological data beat human-only teams in the FAIR weak-lensing challenge. Meanwhile, the new QED system (arXiv 2604.24021) tackles open mathematical proofs with seven purpose-built agents that neutralize classic failure modes like citation hallucination and proof-plan instability, already delivering three verified novel proofs in applied analysis. On the prompting front, the 2026 field guide from SurePrompts overturns 2023 dogma: chain-of-thought hand-holding no [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating UAP disclosure through multiple fronts. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna's Task Force on Declassification continues investigating the Pentagon's withholding of UAP videos, demanding footage from Secretary Hegseth that whistleblowers say AARO possesses. Rep. Tim Burchett, after classified briefings with "every alphabet agency," claims the government has "100%" made contact with non-human intelligence and warns the truth would "unglue" the nation. A mysterious March 25 classified briefing in a congressional SCIF now centers on the disappearance of retired Air Force General Neil McCasland, who vanished days after Trump's UAP disclosure announcement. The 2026 NDAA includes provisions forcing Northcom and NORAD to brief Congress on UAP intercepts, while the UAP Transparency Act (HR1187) seeks full declassification. Multiple lawmakers report being briefed on recovered non-human craft and biologics in secure facilities, with Senator Marco Rubio among those receiving testimony o [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hit me [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.text: The leaders of an Iranian opposition party in exile gathered for a celebratory picnic at Lake Balboa Park in Los Angeles's San Fernando Valley on Easter Sunday. The event was held by the Constitutionalist Party of Iran (CPI), a group that has lived in the US since his father was deposed during the Islamic Revolution of 1979. Pahlavi has maintained a dedicated base of support in LA, where he is seen as a "transitional" leader for Iran. The group's weekly protests have attracted thousands of peopl

enriched_articles.title: US DHS to vet immigrants for what it calls extremist views, raising free speech concerns
enriched_articles.text: The US Department of Homeland Security has begun vetting immigrants for what it calls extremist views, raising significant free speech concerns among civil liberties advocates. The policy directs immigration officials to scrutinize applicants' social media posts and past political statements for signs of what the department deems extremist ideology, potentially including criticism of US foreign policy or support for Palestinian rights. Critics argue this represents an unconstitutional infringeme

enriched_articles.title: Live updates: US-Iran ceasefire talks stall as UAE says it will leave OPEC
enriched_articles.text: US-Iran ceasefire negotiations have stalled as the United Arab Emirates announced it will leave OPEC, dealing a significant blow to the oil cartel's influence. The UAE's decision comes amid ongoing tensions over the Strait of Hormuz blockade, with oil prices continuing to surge above $100 per barrel. President Trump is reportedly reviewing military options as diplomatic efforts appear to have reached an impasse.

enriched_articles.title: Russian superyacht sails through Strait of Hormuz despite blockade
enriched_articles.text: A superyacht linked to Russian billionaire Alexey Mordashov has sailed through the Strait of Hormuz despite the ongoing US blockade. The 465-foot luxury vessel Nord, valued at $500 million, transited from Dubai to Oman over the weekend, highlighting how some vessels are still managing to navigate the restricted waterway. The incident underscores the challenges facing the US naval blockade as Iran continues to restrict shipping through the vital strait.

enriched_articles.title: Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys
enriched_articles.text: Millions of people are now asking chatbots to summarize books, draft emails, and even explain political events to them. But what looks from one perspective like a productivity revolution may also be something more discomfiting: the quiet outsourcing of judgment itself. Writers on artificial intelligence have long claimed that it poses an existential risk because it may become so powerful that it turns against human beings. But AI may create a different kind of existential risk, as philosopher Ni
